content.body: Noam Rosenthal, annevk: it looks like neither Chrome nor Safari does [this](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/#navigate-fragid:~:text=For%20other%20fragment%20navigations%2C%20including%20user%2Dinitiated%20ones%2C%20the%20navigation%20API%20state%20is%20carried%20over%20from%20the%20previous%20entry). is there a reason for that?
content.formatted_body: Noam Rosenthal, annevk: it looks like neither Chrome nor Safari does this. is there a reason for that?

content.body:
and if we don't want carry over to be the default for non-Navigation API navigations, should we add a 
```
enum NavigationStateLifetimeBehavior {
  "none",
  "carryOver"
}
```
value to the options of the navigation api methods (including updateCurrentEntry)?
content.formatted_body:
and if we don't want carry over to be the default for non-Navigation API navigations, should we add a

enum NavigationStateLifetimeBehavior {
  "none",
  "carryOver"
}

value to the options of the navigation api methods (including updateCurrentEntry)?

content.body: I'm not sure. Is there a test Chrome and Safari fail?

content.formatted_body: not yet, but soon

content.body: but we've been discussing a bit around what is actually preferable
I suspect you and Jake Archibald are probably a better judge of that than I am.
content.formatted_body: I suspect you and Jake Archibald are probably a better judge of that than I am.

content.body:
So for Ladybird, I have implemented support for downloading `application/octet-stream` resources, and for other content types we don’t support. See <https://github.com/LadybirdBrowser/ladybird/issues/10238>. And now I am at the point of essentially just trying to cleanly retrofit/align a “hook” (as it were) for my already-implemented (conforming) handle-as-a-download behavior with the actual spec requirements.

So I am looking at step 3 and step 4 of https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/browsing-the-web.html#loading-a-document. 

And I am not implementing step 3 at all — because we’re not going to do _“displaying some sort of inline content, e.g., a native rendering of the content or an error message”_.

So OK then I move on to step 4. We’re not going to do _“Hand-off to external software”_. What we‘re doing is just _“handle as a download”._
content.formatted_body:
So for Ladybird, I have implemented support for downloading application/octet-stream resources, and for other content types we don’t support. See https://github.com/LadybirdBrowser/ladybird/issues/10238. And now I am at the point of essentially just trying to cleanly retrofit/align a “hook” (as it were) for my already-implemented (conforming) handle-as-a-download behavior with the actual spec requirements.

So I am looking at step 3 and step 4 of https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/browsing-the-web.html#loading-a-document.

And I am not implementing step 3 at all — because we’re not going to do “displaying some sort of inline content, e.g., a native rendering of the content or an error message”.

So OK then I move on to step 4. We’re not going to do “Hand-off to external software”. What we‘re doing is just “handle as a download”.

content.body:
My problem is: The language for step 3 doesn’t normatively state anything that actually seems to allow me to conformantly do _“handle as a download” in this case.

Instead, the language there just says kind of hand-wavingly alludes to the _possibility_ that there are cases where _“an unknown type that will be processed by handle as a download”_. But never says how to actually normatively make that decision.

So the https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/links.html#handle-as-a-download algorithm is not actually called normatively for this case — though it is for related `Content-Disposition: attachment` case.
content.formatted_body:
My problem is: The language for step 3 doesn’t normatively state anything that actually seems to allow me to conformantly do _“handle as a download” in this case.

Instead, the language there just says kind of hand-wavingly alludes to the possibility that there are cases where “an unknown type that will be processed by handle as a download”. But never says how to actually normatively make that decision.

So the https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/links.html#handle-as-a-download algorithm is not actually called normatively for this case — though it is for related Content-Disposition: attachment case.

content.body:
* My problem is: The language for that step 4 doesn’t normatively state anything that actually seems to allow me to conformantly do \_“handle as a download” in this case.

Instead, the language there just says kind of hand-wavingly alludes to the _possibility_ that there are cases where _“an unknown type that will be processed by handle as a download”_. But never says how to actually normatively make that decision.

So the https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/links.html#handle-as-a-download algorithm is not actually called normatively for this case — though it is for related `Content-Disposition: attachment` case.

So the https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/links.html#handle-as-a-download algorithm is not actually called normatively for this case — though it is for related Content-Disposition: attachment case.

content.body: This was rather hard to follow, but given the quote I think you mean step 4 of https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/browsing-the-web.html#loading-a-document ?
Yeah
* yeah, and I assume Noam Rosenthal also
content.formatted_body: * yeah, and I assume Noam Rosenthal also

m.new_content.body: yeah, and I assume Noam Rosenthal also
m.new_content.formatted_body: yeah, and I assume Noam Rosenthal also

content.body: Ooh I only saw this second post from you somehow. I see there's one more when I scroll up. Oops.
No worries yeah it was a lot of words I as trying to type in a short time 😅

I think we should probably split 4. So we first in an implementation-defined manner check that it goes to external software and if so hand it off and then have a step 5 that's otherwise which calls handle as a download.
Would be good if farre Jake Archibald Noam Rosenthal chimed in as well.
content.formatted_body: Would be good if farre Jake Archibald Noam Rosenthal chimed in as well.

content.body: (The problem I had btw was that the given link preview windows pushed your earlier message out of my screen and I assumed you had only typed one message since I saw you were typing one. Funny that.)
Element UX is … not ideal

Anyway yeah what you said about splitting would certainly seem to make sense. What I can say implementation-wise at least is: The user behavior (described in the issue I linked to) has caused our implementation of https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/browsing-the-web.html#loading-a-document to get hit. And unless we make _some_ change, then we end up just spinning there in the way way the user describes.

So, we’ve gotta do _something_ here. We’re already in that code, and we’re at that point, and we’ve gotta do something different.

We know we are not going to hand off to an external app. And we know we are not going to try to display the content inline. We _could_ display an error message. But then we’d just be (unnecessarily) completely blocking the user from getting (to) the resource.

So we already _know_ we want to just download the resource for the user. And I _can_ do that (and already implemented it). It’s just a question of kind of trying to make that OK as far as the spec requirements go.
content.formatted_body:
Anyway yeah what you said about splitting would certainly seem to make sense. What I can say implementation-wise at least is: The user behavior (described in the issue I linked to) has caused our implementation of https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/browsing-the-web.html#loading-a-document to get hit. And unless we make some change, then we end up just spinning there in the way way the user describes.

So, we’ve gotta do something here. We’re already in that code, and we’re at that point, and we’ve gotta do something different.

We know we are not going to hand off to an external app. And we know we are not going to try to display the content inline. We could display an error message. But then we’d just be (unnecessarily) completely blocking the user from getting (to) the resource.

So we already know we want to just download the resource for the user. And I can do that (and already implemented it). It’s just a question of kind of trying to make that OK as far as the spec requirements go.

content.body: hsivonen: perhaps the URL standard should name its operations "string to ASCII domain" and "domain to Unicode" for maximum clarity
content.formatted_body: hsivonen: perhaps the URL standard should name its operations "string to ASCII domain" and "domain to Unicode" for maximum clarity

content.body: It would be a pretty straightforward specification change that I think we would accept as a bug fix as this is hard to test regardless. Do you want to make it?

I don't have lots of input, other than that I agree that the spec text is vague. for us the external handler also more or less also does handle as a download as part of it's implementation, so I'm not entirely sure if we'd need to adapt to such a spec change. smaug might know more.
content.formatted_body: I don't have lots of input, other than that I agree that the spec text is vague. for us the external handler also more or less also does handle as a download as part of it's implementation, so I'm not entirely sure if we'd need to adapt to such a spec change. smaug might know more.

content.body: would we make the handle as a download step optional? otherwise it looks like we'd change behaviour here
I was thinking it would always be handled as a download as final step. I think handled as a download still allows you to just return early though.
Would allow-downloads vs allow-top-navigation-to-custom-protocols matter here
Hmm yeah, I suppose so. If handle as a download doesn't handle those I suppose there might be further issues.
And it doesn't seem like it does so meh.
One of the big complaint about the history API was that state was lost just because the user clicked an internal page link
The navigation API was intended to avoid this footgun

Was this explicitly for the frag nav case or the general case? Because this means that all navigations via < a href ="#foo" > now will clone the state regardless (and the user has no control). There was a bug filed from a user who was surprised to see that navigation.navigate("#foo") and < a href="#foo" > did not lead to the same behavior which started this whole thing.
annevk: Do you intend to make it so that you wouldn't pass raw input to either one, but you'd have to (at least conceptually) pass the output of the former as input to the latter?
content.formatted_body: annevk: Do you intend to make it so that you wouldn't pass raw input to either one, but you'd have to (at least conceptually) pass the output of the former as input to the latter?

content.body: hsivonen: that is already the case, "domain to ASCII" takes a string, "domain to Unicode" takes a domain
content.formatted_body: hsivonen: that is already the case, "domain to ASCII" takes a string, "domain to Unicode" takes a domain

content.body: annevk: Well, then the current naming is indeed bad.
content.formatted_body: annevk: Well, then the current naming is indeed bad.

content.body: annevk: And what's the point of _beStrict_ in the "domain to Unicode" operation, then?
content.formatted_body: annevk: And what's the point of beStrict in the "domain to Unicode" operation, then?

content.body: When reading the spec, I have managed to miss that "domain" is a strongly-typed thing distinct from a string that's used for domain purpose.

I think _beStrict_ is indeed meaningless as it is already enforced when a string becomes a domain. I don't think I quite had that realization before as domain to Unicode also signified validation errors which is not needed. Not sure.
content.formatted_body: I think beStrict is indeed meaningless as it is already enforced when a string becomes a domain. I don't think I quite had that realization before as domain to Unicode also signified validation errors which is not needed. Not sure.

content.body: turn of events: looks like `location.hash = "#foo"` actually _will_ carry over in Chrome
content.formatted_body: turn of events: looks like location.hash = "#foo" actually will carry over in Chrome

content.body: Yea I remember it working correctly last time I checked
It doesn't carry over if you navigate programmatically via the history/nav API, only if the user navigates
* It doesn't carry over if you navigate programmatically via the history/nav API, only if the user navigates or with the other APIs. But I am sure there could be edge cases here as always
